elements.sent-2411-001: Let $F$ be a $p$-adic field containing the full group of $n^{th}$ roots of 1 and let $\msl$ be the $n$-fold cover of $SL_2(F)$ constructed by Kubota.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper we compute the dimension of the space of Whittaker functionals of the two irreducible summands inside a reducible unitary genuine principal series representation of $\msl$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also show how these dimensions change when the Whittaker character is modified.
elements.sent-2411-004: As an application we determine the action of the twisted Kazhdan-Patterson $n$-fold cover of $GL_2(F)$ on the two summands.
elements.sent-2411-005: We emphasize that our main results addresses both ramified and unramified representations and do not rely on the assumption that the cover is tame.
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $G$ be a quasi-split reductive group defined over a $p$-field $F$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Fix a Borel subgroup ${ B ={ H \ltimes { N}$ and a non-degenerate character $\psi$ of $N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Let $M$ be a Levi subgroup of $G$ containing $H$ and let $\psi'$ be the restriction of $\psi$ to $N\cap M$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Let $\sigma$ be a smooth admissible irreducible representation of $M$ and let $Wh_{\psi' (\sigma)$ be the space of $\psi'$-Whittaker functionals on $\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The well known uniqueness of Whittaker model stats that if $\sigma$ is $\psi'$-generic then $\dim Wh_{\psi' (\sigma) = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Let $\pi=I(\sigma)$ be the representation of $G$ parabolically induced from $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Rodier Heredity implies that if $\sigma$ is $\psi'$-generic then $\dim Wh_\psi(\pi)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, if $\pi$ is reducible then exactly one of its irreducible constituents is $\psi$-generic.
elements.sent-2411-014: This situation is changed dramatically when we move to covering groups.
elements.sent-2411-015: For these groups, uniqueness of Whittaker model rarely holds, see \cite{GSS .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In recent years the problem of determining the space of Whittaker functionals of various representations of covering groups has been studied extensively.
elements.sent-2411-018: If the uniqueness of Whittaker model fails for a certain covering group, it fails already for its genuine principal series representations.
elements.sent-2411-019: The goal of this paper is to determine the dimensions of the space of Whittaker functionals for the irreducible constitutions of reducible unitary genuine principal series representations of $\msl=\widetilde{SL_2(F)}$, the $n$-fold cover of $SL_2(F)$ defined in \cite{Kub .}
elements.sent-2411-020: We emphasize that our results apply to both ramified and unramified representations and to any Whittaker character.
elements.sent-2411-021: We also emphasize that we do not rely on the assumption that the cover is tame.
elements.sent-2411-022: Namely, we do not assume that $gcd(n,p)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Such reducible representations of $\msl$ exists only if $n$ is odd, see \cite{Sz19 , Proposition 5.5 and Section 5.7.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Similar to the linear case, the set of reducible unitary genuine principal series representations of $\msl$ is parameterized by the set of non-trivial quadratic characters of $F^*$, see Section \ref{planchsec below.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Let $\pi$ be a reducible genuine unitary principal series representation of $\msl$.
elements.sent-2411-026: It is a sum of two non-isomorphic irreducible representations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Since $\dim Wh_\psi(\pi)$ is odd we may write $\pi=\pi^{+ _\psi \oplus \pi^{- _\psi$ where the irreducible summands $\pi^{\pm _\psi$ are defined by the relation \begin{equation}\dim Wh_\psi (\pi^{+}_\psi)>\dim Wh_\psi (\pi^{-}_\psi).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our main result given in Section \ref{mainsec , Theorem \ref{mainthm , states that \begin{equation}\dim Wh_\psi (\pi^{+}_\psi)=\ab n \ab^{-\half} \frac{n+1}{2}, \, \, \dim Wh_\psi (\pi^{-}_\psi)=\ab n \ab^{-\half} \frac{n-1}{2}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Here $\ab \cdot \ab$ is the usual $p$-adic absolute value.
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, if one changes the Whittaker character to $\psi_c$ where $c \in F^*$ (see the body of the paper for details) then \begin{equation}\dim Wh_{\psi_c} (\pi^{+}_\psi)=\ab n \ab^{-\half} \frac{n+\theta(c)}{2}, \, \, \dim Wh_{\psi_c} (\pi^{-}_\psi)=\ab n \ab^{-\half} \frac{n-\theta(c)}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: Here $\theta$ is the non-trivial quadratic character of $F^*$ associated with $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-032: We now give an outline of the proof of Theorem 1: first we normalize the standard intertwining operator by a suitable $\gamma$-factor so that the normalized operator is a $\msl$ isomorphism of order 2.
elements.sent-2411-033: We then use the normalized operator to construct the projections from $Wh_\psi(\pi)$ to $Wh_\psi(\pi_\psi^{\pm )$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The last step in the proof is a computation of the rank of these projections.
elements.sent-2411-035: The computation ultimately boils down to a computation of the trace of a certain scattering matrix.
elements.sent-2411-036: A by-product of our proof is that $\pi_\psi^{\pm}$ are identified as the $\pm 1$ eigenspaces of the normalized intertwining operator.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our results in the unramified case are among Gao's results in \cite{Gao2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The normalization factor in that work of Gao is the one coming from the Gindikin-Karpelevich formula and his computation of the trace uses the explicit description of the scattering matrices obtained by McNamara in \cite{Mc2 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Both Gindikin-Karpelevich formula and McNamara's work are applicable for unramified representations only.
elements.sent-2411-040: In fact McNamara assumes that the Whittaker character is normalized.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our first novelty is the usage the of the explicit computation of the Plancherel measure in \cite{Sz19 , Theorem 5.7 to obtain the normalization factor, see Section \ref{planchsec below.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In the unramified case it agrees with Gao's.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our second novelty is the usage of the coarse description of the local coefficients matrices in \cite{Sz19 , Theorem 4.12 to compute the trace.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Using the formalism from \cite{Sz19 , this last computation turns out to be very simple, in fact it is nothing but Fourier inversion for finite abelian groups, see Section \ref{scatsec .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-046: First, in Section \ref{presec we introduce some known notions and results regarding the $p$-adic field $F$ and the covering group $\msl$ along with some material on its principal series representations.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Our main result is presented in Section \ref{mainresec .}
elements.sent-2411-048: We have included two remarks in the end of Section \ref{mainsec : in Remark \ref{mainrem we discuss the analogy to the linear case and the relation to the theory of $R$-groups.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Remark \ref{Sharem addresses the relation between the normalization factor and the Shahidi-type invariant defined in \cite{GSS .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section \ref{glsec we use our main result to determine the action of the Kazhdan-Patterson twisted $n$-fold cover of $GL_2(F)$ on $\pi_\psi^{\pm}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, in Section \ref{unramsec we use this action along with our main result to study the unramified case.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Let $F$ be a finite extension of $\Q_p$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Denote by $q$ the cardinality of its residue field.
elements.sent-2411-054: Denote by $\Of$ its ring of integers.
elements.sent-2411-055: Fix $\varpi$, a generator of $\Pf$, the maximal ideal of $\Of$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We normalize the absolute value on $F$ such that $\ab \varpi \ab =q^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $\psi$ be a non-trivial character of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We define $e(\psi)$, the conductor of $\psi$, to be the smallest integer $k$ such that $\psi$ is trivial on $\Pf^k$.
elements.sent-2411-059: For $c \in F^*$ let $\psi_c$ be the character of $F$ given by $x \mapsto \psi(cx)$.
elements.sent-2411-060: The map $\psi\mapsto \psi_c$ is an isomorphism from $F$ to its Pontryagin dual $\widehat{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: We denote by $d_\psi$ the $\psi$-self dual Haar measure on $F$.
elements.sent-2411-063: If $\chi$ is ramified we define its conductor, $e(\chi)$, to be the smallest $k\in \N$ such that $\chi$ is trivial on $1+\Pf^k$.
elements.sent-2411-065: For $s\in \C$ we set $\chi_s=\ab \cdot \ab^s\chi.$ In this paper $\theta$ will denote a non-trivial quadratic character of $F^*$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Tate $\gamma$-factor associated with $\chi$, $\psi$ and $s\in \C$ is defined by \begin{equation}\gamma(s,\chi,\psi)=\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi)\frac{L(1-s,\chi^{-1})}{L(s,\chi)}\end{equation} where $\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi)$ is a monomial function in $q^{-s}$ satisfying \begin{eqnarray}\label{Tate gamma} {\epsilon}(1-s,\chi^{-1},\psi) = \chi(-1)\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi)^{-1}, \label{changepsi}\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi_c) = \chi(c)\ab c \ab^{s-\half}\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi), \label{epsilon old twist} \epsilon(s+t,\chi,\psi) = q^{e(\psi)-e(\chi)t}\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi),\end{eqnarray} We note that if $e(\psi)=0$ and $\chi$ is unramified then $\epsilon(s,\chi,\psi)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-067: For all these assertions see Section 1 in \cite{Schmidt for example.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Combining \eqref{Tate gamma and \eqref{epsilon old twist one obtains \begin{equation}\label{epsilon twist and inv} \epsilon(1-s,\chi^{-1},\psi)\epsilon(1+s,\chi,\psi)= \chi(-1)q^{e(\psi)-e(\chi)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We shall assume in this paper that $F^*$ contains the full group of $n^{th}$ roots of 1.
elements.sent-2411-072: We identify $\mu_n$ with the group of $n^{th}$ roots of 1 in $\C^*$ and suppress this identification.
elements.sent-2411-073: Let \begin{equation}( \cdot, \cdot):F^* \times F^* \rightarrow \mu_{n}\end{equation} be the $n^{th}$ power Hilbert symbol.
elements.sent-2411-074: Recall that it is an anti-symmetric bilinear form and that its kernel in each argument is ${F^* ^{n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Hence, it gives rise to a non-degenerate bilinear form on $F^* / {F^* ^{n \times F^* / {F^* ^{n}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The group $\widehat{F^* / {F^* ^{n }$ may also be identified with the group of characters of $F^*$ whose order divide $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: For $x \in F^*$ let $\eta_x$ be the character of $F^*$ defined by \begin{equation}y \mapsto \eta_x(y)=(x,y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: When convenient we shall also think of $\eta_x$ as an element in $\widehat{F^*/{F^* ^n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: A subgroup $\overline{J}$ and of $F^*/{F^* ^n$ is called a maximal isotropic subgroup if \begin{equation}\overline{J} =\bigcap_{x \in {\overline{J}}} \operatorname{ker}(\eta_x).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-081: By \cite{Sz19 , Lemma 2.3, there exist two maximal isotropic subgroups $\overline{J}$ and $\overline{K}$ of $F^*/{F^* ^n$ such that.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The map $k \mapsto {\eta_k \! \mid _{\overline{J }$ is an isomorphism from $\overline{K}$ to the Pontryagin dual of $\overline{J}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In the case where $gcd(n,p)=1$, the standard choice in the literature is \begin{equation}(\overline{J},\overline{K})=(\Of^* {{ F}^*}^n / {{ F}^*}^n, <\varpi> {{ F}^*}^n / {{ F}^*}^n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: The following partial $\gamma$-factor was defined in \cite{Sz19 , Page 127, \begin{equation}\gamma_{_J}(s,\chi,\psi,k)={(\# \overline{J})}^{-1} \sum_{j \in \overline{J}} \gamma(s,\chi\eta_j,\psi)\eta_k(j).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-087: See \cite{Sz19 , Theorem 2.12 for the motivation for this definition.}
elements.sent-2411-088: By Fourier inversion for finite abelian groups we have \begin{equation}\label{invert} \sum_{k \in \overline{K}}\gamma_{_J}(s,\chi,\psi,k)=\gamma(s,\chi,\psi).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: Most of the material in this section and in Sections \ref{torussec , \ref{prinsec and \ref{whidefsec is well known.}}}

elements.sent-2411-092: Let ${ N \simeq { F}$ be the group of upper triangular unipotent matrices in $G$ and let ${ H \simeq { F ^*$ be the group of diagonal elements inside $G$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-137: A $\psi$-Whittaker functional on a representation $(\pi,V_\pi)$ of $\msl$ is a functional on $V_\pi$ satisfying \begin{equation}\xi\bigl(\pi \bigl(u \bigr)v \bigr)=\psi(u)\xi(v)\end{equation} for all $v \in V_\pi$, $u\in N$.
